data.title: Is Church & Dwight (CHD) Still Appealing After A 15.7% Year To Date Gain?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Church & Dwight at around US$95.63 still offers value, you are not alone. This article focuses squarely on what that price might mean for long term investors.
  • The stock is roughly flat over the past week and month, each down about 0.6%. Year to date it is up 15.7%, and over the last year it is down 1.5%, which together hint at shifting views on both its potential and its risks.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Church & Dwight's position within the Household Products sector and on how investors are reacting to consumer staples stocks more broadly. This gives context to why the share price has moved modestly in the short term yet still sits higher year to date. This backdrop matters because valuation often reflects how investors weigh the stability of everyday brands against other opportunities in the market.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Church & Dwight scores 2 out of 6, which suggests some valuation metrics look more appealing than others. Next comes a closer look at the different valuation approaches and, finally, a broader framework that can help you make sense of them.

Church & Dwight scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Church & Dwight Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a company may generate in the future and discounts those back into today’s dollars, aiming to arrive at an intrinsic value per share.

For Church & Dwight, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.02b. Analysts and extrapolated estimates point to projected free cash flow of $1.23b by 2030, with interim projections between 2026 and 2035 ranging from roughly $1.03b to $1.45b in annual free cash flow, all expressed in dollar terms.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the resulting intrinsic value from the DCF is $125.34 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about $95.63, the model output indicates Church & Dwight is trading at a 23.7% discount to this estimate on this cash flow view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Church & Dwight is undervalued by 23.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 46 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Church & Dwight.

Approach 2: Church & Dwight Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It lines up directly with how quickly earnings might grow and how much risk you are taking on, which together influence what could be seen as a normal or fair P/E for a stock.

Church & Dwight currently trades on a P/E of 30.9x. This sits above the Household Products industry average of 16.3x and also above the peer average of 20.4x, which suggests the stock carries a higher earnings multiple than many sector peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Church & Dwight is 18.7x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be given factors such as the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and identified risks. Because it blends these company specific drivers with the broader context, it can often be more informative than looking only at raw industry or peer averages.

Comparing the current P/E of 30.9x with the Fair Ratio of 18.7x points to the stock trading above this fair value range on an earnings multiple view.

Result: OVERVALUED

Q1 2026 earnings snapshot for Ernst Russ (XTRA:ERAG)

Ernst Russ (XTRA:ERAG) has opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of €41.0 million and net income of €11.0 million, setting a fresh reference point for investors watching the stock at around €9.10. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from €74.2 million in Q1 2025 to €41.0 million in Q1 2026, while trailing twelve month net income sits at €59.8 million against €42.5 million two years ago. This gives helpful context for how the latest quarter fits into the recent earnings path. With trailing net profit margins currently well above last year’s level, this update gives investors a clearer read on how much of that profitability is holding up in the current run rate.

See our full analysis for Ernst Russ.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the dominant narratives around Ernst Russ, including views on growth durability, risk, and valuation signals.

See what the community is saying about Ernst Russ

47.9% net margin versus shrinking forecasts

  • Over the last 12 months, Ernst Russ earned €59.8 million on €124.9 million of revenue, which works out to a 47.9% net profit margin compared with 26.6% a year earlier.
  • Bears point to forecasts that earnings could decline by about 29.6% per year over the next three years, yet
    • current margins of 47.9% and trailing earnings growth of 12.3% over the last year sit against that expectation of lower future profits and margins moving toward 11.9% in three years, creating a clear gap between recent profitability and the cautious view, and
    • five year earnings growth of 26.4% per year provides a longer track record that skeptics contrast with the forecast contraction from €73.5 million of recent earnings to €16.9 million by around 2029.

Low 5.1x P/E against DCF fair value of €73.04

  • With the share price at €9.10, the stock trades on a 5.1x P/E compared with 14.9x for the industry and 27.1x for peers, and it sits far below the €73.04 DCF fair value used in the analysis.
  • For bullish investors, the combination of low multiples and high reported profitability heavily supports the idea of a valuation gap, yet
    • the same data set includes forecasts for earnings to decline by 29.6% per year over three years, which challenges the assumption that current margins and returns will persist, and
    • the DCF fair value of €73.04 and an analyst price target of €13.23 both sit above the current €9.10 share price, which bulls cite as support while bears focus on the earnings and margin compression embedded in those models.
On top of these headline ratios, bulls argue that long term charter contracts and a high equity ratio could help support cash flows even if earnings forecasts prove conservative, so it can be useful to compare this earnings print with the full bullish case in 🐂 Ernst Russ Bull Case.

Strong five year earnings growth, but quality flags

  • Over the past five years, earnings grew by 26.4% per year and by 12.3% over the last 12 months, yet the analysis also flags a high level of non cash items in reported earnings and an unstable dividend record.
  • Critics highlight these quality concerns as key to the bearish argument, because
    • a high share of non cash earnings means the €59.8 million of trailing net income may not all translate into cash that can be reinvested or paid out, which bears see as a risk to sustaining the 47.9% margin, and
    • the unstable dividend description sits awkwardly alongside the strong five year earnings growth record, prompting questions from cautious investors about how consistently these profits have supported shareholder returns.
Skeptical investors increasingly ask whether the past five year earnings expansion and current 47.9% margin can offset concerns about non cash items and forecast contraction, so it is worth seeing how the full cautious case frames these risks in 🐻 Ernst Russ Bear Case.

data.title: Whale Rock Backs MKS As AI Infrastructure Supply Chain Attracts Attention
data.description:
• Whale Rock Capital Management has opened a substantial new position in NasdaqGS:MKSI.
  • The move reflects a shift in its portfolio toward suppliers of AI infrastructure.
  • This stake highlights fresh institutional attention on companies that support AI hardware and manufacturing.

NasdaqGS:MKSI is a supplier of equipment and technologies used in advanced electronics manufacturing, including areas tied to AI data centers and high performance computing. With more capital flowing into AI infrastructure, attention is widening from headline AI stocks to companies that provide the tools and components behind them. Whale Rock's new position places MKS within that group of suppliers.

For investors, this new holding can be a data point when assessing how professional money is treating the AI supply chain. It adds an angle to the MKS story that sits alongside valuation and share price behavior, and it may prompt a closer look at how the business is positioned in AI related end markets.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for MKS by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on MKS.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling MKS following this latest news.

Whale Rock Capital Management’s new position in MKS puts a well-followed hedge fund on the same side of the AI-infrastructure trade that many retail investors are watching. Whale Rock has shifted capital away from Nvidia toward suppliers such as MKS, Advanced Energy Industries and Viavi Solutions, a sign that some institutional investors are looking further down the supply chain to companies providing tools, power systems and testing equipment for AI data centers and high-performance computing. For you, the signal is less about one fund’s view on MKS and more about where sophisticated capital is currently hunting for exposure to AI hardware buildout. However, a fresh hedge fund position does not remove company specific issues such as cyclicality in semiconductor equipment, leverage or competitive pressure from peers like Applied Materials, Lam Research or Entegris. It is one piece of sentiment, not a verdict on valuation or risk. The key question is whether this interest lines up with your own view on MKS’s role in AI related manufacturing and your tolerance for swings linked to capex cycles.

How This Fits Into The MKS Narrative

  • Whale Rock’s focus on AI-infrastructure suppliers lines up with the narrative that growing AI and high-performance computing demand is supporting MKS’s semiconductor and electronics packaging segments.
  • The increased attention on AI-related upside may make it easier to overlook existing concerns in the narrative around leverage, tariff exposure and cyclicality in wafer fab equipment spending.
  • The specific signal that a large hedge fund is rotating from headline AI stocks into infrastructure suppliers is not fully captured in the narrative, which mainly centers on end market demand and company execution.

  • ⚠️ Analysts have already flagged concerns around leverage and interest coverage, so higher expectations from new institutional holders could increase sensitivity to any earnings disappointment.
  • 🎁 Whale Rock’s move suggests some professional investors see AI hardware suppliers such as MKS, Advanced Energy Industries and Viavi Solutions as important ways to gain exposure to AI related spending.
  • 🎁 Additional institutional attention can increase liquidity and put more focus on how MKS executes on semiconductor, electronics and packaging opportunities tied to AI data centers.

What To Watch Going Forward

After this stake, keep an eye on future regulatory filings to see whether Whale Rock and other large holders are adding to or trimming MKS, as that can give extra context on sentiment around the AI-infrastructure theme. It is also worth tracking how MKS reports on orders and backlog in semiconductor and advanced packaging, since those areas sit at the heart of the thesis that AI related manufacturing needs will support equipment suppliers. data.title: Constellation Energy Extends Clean Portfolio With Pine Creek Renewable Gas Bet
data.description:
• Constellation Energy (NasdaqGS:CEG) has entered a long-term agreement with Pine Creek RNG.
  • The company is acquiring a minority equity interest in five renewable natural gas production facilities.
  • The move marks an expansion of Constellation Energy's clean energy portfolio into renewable natural gas.

Constellation Energy, trading at a recent share price of $287.75, is primarily known for its nuclear and renewables portfolio. The stock has seen a very large gain over 3 years, while being down 21.4% year to date and down 5.5% over the past year. This new renewable natural gas exposure adds another clean energy source to the mix at a time when the stock has pulled back from earlier highs.

The long-term agreement with Pine Creek RNG gives Constellation Energy direct involvement in renewable natural gas production assets. Investors following NasdaqGS:CEG may watch how this new segment fits alongside the existing generation portfolio and how it affects earnings and customer offerings over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Constellation Energy by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Constellation Energy.

We've flagged 3 risks for Constellation Energy. See which could impact your investment.

This move into renewable natural gas gives Constellation Energy another way to serve large customers that want lower-carbon energy options alongside nuclear and renewables. Because Constellation is only taking a minority equity stake in the Pine Creek RNG assets, it gains exposure to this part of the value chain without taking on full project risk. For investors, the key questions are how meaningful this segment could become relative to the existing fleet and whether it supports Constellation’s efforts to win or retain long-duration contracts with corporates and data center operators. The agreement also slightly broadens the company’s fuel mix at a time when peers such as NextEra Energy, Duke Energy and Dominion Energy are also adjusting portfolios in response to customer decarbonization targets and regulatory scrutiny. Execution will matter, including how reliably these facilities operate, how contract structures are set up and whether Constellation can build on this initial step with further projects or partnerships over time.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Constellation Energy Narrative

  • The Pine Creek RNG agreement aligns with the narrative focus on long-term, higher-margin contracts for carbon-free and lower-carbon power. It may add another product that can be bundled into 24/7 carbon-free offerings.
  • The move slightly challenges the narrative’s emphasis on nuclear and centralized assets by introducing a different type of asset. This could complicate capital allocation and operational priorities if not managed carefully.
  • Renewable natural gas as a distinct product set is not a major feature of the existing narrative, so the potential impact on customer mix, margins and earnings volatility may not be fully reflected yet.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Constellation Energy to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Investors already face three flagged risks for Constellation Energy, including balance sheet and dilution concerns, and this new RNG exposure adds execution risk around a relatively new asset class.
  • ⚠️ If renewable natural gas policy support or credit markets weaken, returns from these facilities could be lower than expected, especially given Constellation’s minority position.
  • 🎁 The agreement can support Constellation’s positioning as a provider of cleaner energy solutions to large customers that want diversified low-carbon options, which may strengthen contract discussions.
  • 🎁 Adding RNG to a portfolio that includes nuclear, gas and solar could modestly diversify earnings sources and reduce reliance on any single technology or regulatory framework.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on how Constellation reports contributions from the Pine Creek RNG assets, including volumes sold, contract structures and any commentary on customer demand for renewable natural gas. It is also worth tracking whether management references RNG more often when discussing long-duration contracts with data centers and corporates, which would signal growing commercial relevance. Finally, compare Constellation’s progress in low-carbon offerings against utilities such as NextEra Energy, Duke Energy and Dominion Energy to see whether this step helps the company stay competitive in serving large buyers with decarbonization goals.

Together, these figures give you a clear snapshot of where the income statement currently stands. Over recent quarters, revenue has moved from US$3.1 million in Q4 2024 to between US$4.0 million and US$9.2 million through 2025. Quarterly basic EPS shifted from a loss of US$0.10 in Q4 2024 to positive readings between US$0.00 and US$0.04. This sets up the latest result as part of a broader earnings reset. For investors, the mix of higher net income and reported profitability over the past year points to margins that are now firmly in focus.

  • What stands out for a bullish view is that reported profit has grown at 28.3% per year over five years, yet this sits against a history of TTM losses as recently as early 2025. Investors who lean optimistic are likely to pay close attention to whether this new US$8.4 million profit is being backed up by repeatable operations or is flattered by the high level of non cash items flagged as a key earnings quality risk.

    • The gap between the 12.4x P/E and the wider industry at 24.4x can be seen as a discount compared with other Canadian Oil & Gas stocks, which may catch the eye of investors focused on valuation metrics.
    • However, the premium to the 7.9x peer average aligns with the view that the market is already pricing in the recent move to TTM profitability, so anyone using the P/E has to balance that against the earlier periods of TTM losses.

Approach 1: Raymond James Financial Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how efficiently Raymond James Financial uses shareholder capital, then compares those returns to the cost of funding that capital. Instead of focusing on short term earnings, it asks whether each dollar of equity is generating more value than it costs.

For Raymond James Financial, the model uses a Book Value of $64.56 per share and a Stable EPS of $13.81 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 6 analysts. The Average Return on Equity is 18.67%. Against this, the Cost of Equity is $6.09 per share, which implies an Excess Return of $7.72 per share. Combining these inputs, the Excess Returns framework produces an estimated intrinsic value of $238.89 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $143.41, this implies the stock is 40.0% undervalued on this model.

Approach 2: Raymond James Financial Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to link what you pay for each share to the earnings that support it. It helps you see how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a "normal" P/E usually reflects how investors see the trade off between growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk tends to line up with a lower P/E.
